title: Chapter 36: The East Wing
content: Smoke didn't rise the way I expected it to.

I thought smoke climbed — linear, obedient, seeking the ceiling like something polite. But this smoke crawled along the walls and pressed against the floor and coiled around my ankles like it was hunting me. It filled the corridor outside the Great Hall in a rolling bank, grey turning black at the edges, and I couldn't see more than three feet in front of my face.
The sprinklers had failed. Or maybe they were still going somewhere deeper in the building — I could hear water, that thin metallic pattering, but it wasn't enough. It was a whisper against a scream.

The heat was a living thing. It pressed against my skin from every direction, not like standing near a fire but like standing inside one's memory. The walls were hot when I touched them — instinct, reaching out for balance when the smoke got thick — and I yanked my hand back, palm stinging.
Then hands grabbed my arms.
I nearly drove my elbow into a throat before I registered the face through the smoke.
Heath.

His eyes were bloodshot, wet with tears that weren't grief but reflex — the body's useless protest against air that had turned to poison. His hair was damp, plastered to his forehead, and there was soot streaked across one cheekbone like war paint applied by a child.
We held onto each other. Not gently. Our fingers dug in like we were the last two solid things in the world and neither of us trusted the other not to dissolve.
"We need to go," he said, and his voice was shredded, a rasp over gravel. "The east wing is—"
"Where's Julian?"
The question left my mouth before I'd finished forming it. It wasn't strategy. It wasn't calculation. It was something older and dumber and more honest than anything I'd thought in years.

Heath's grip tightened on my arms. His face did something complicated — a flicker, a contraction, a hardening. I watched him subtract Julian from the equation in real time, the way he'd been subtracting people from equations his whole life, the way I'd taught myself to do the same.
Julian was not his priority.
Julian had to be.
"We didn't do all of this," I said, "just to let an innocent person burn."

The words sounded naive the moment they left my mouth. Innocent. Like that word had any currency here, in the halls where innocence came to die. But I said it anyway, because it was true, and because the alternative was becoming exactly what Blackwood had always wanted me to be.
Heath released my arms. Not gently — he pushed me back half an inch, like he needed distance to think clearly. He wiped his mouth with the back of his hand and left a dark smear across his jaw.
"The east wing," he said. "Julian went to the east wing."
Not a question. A statement of fact, delivered with the flat certainty of someone who already knew the answer and hated it.
"Why?" I asked, but I already knew too.

Because Julian was Julian. Because he'd gone looking for evidence — more documents, more proof, more of the paper trail that had started all of this. He'd gone to be thorough, to be careful, to do the right thing down to the last footnote while the building burned around him. That was who he was. The good soldier. The meticulous, careful, decent human being who could not — could not — prioritize his own survival over doing what was correct.
It would be admirable if it weren't so infuriating.
It would be beautiful if it weren't going to kill him.

I turned toward the east wing corridor before I could think about it, and the heat hit me like a wall. Not a door — a wall. The air itself was solid, pressing against my chest, refusing to let me breathe. The corridor ahead was a tunnel of smoke laced with orange, the ceiling lost somewhere above in a churning black-red sky that didn't belong indoors.

Flames licked along the baseboards on the left side, blue at the core and yellow at the edges, and I could hear them — a low, continuous roar like a freight train parked inside the walls, punctuated by cracks and pops as wood surrendered its shape. The sound of a building digesting itself.
Somewhere inside that tunnel, Julian was looking for evidence.
I took a step forward and Heath caught my wrist.
"Don't," he said.
"He's in there."
"I know he's in there." His voice was barely a voice now — just air scraped raw. "I know."
"Then we—"
"We leave. Now. While we still can."
I turned to face him fully. The smoke was thick between us, turning his outline into something impressionistic, all soft edges and hard eyes.
"He's in there because of us," I said. "Because of what we started."
"No. He's in there because he's an idiot who can't prioritize his own survival over doing the right thing."
"And that's exactly why we have to get him out."

Heath stared at me. I could see the calculus running behind his eyes, the cost-benefit analysis he performed on every human interaction like it was a quarterly report. Julian in one column. Risk in another. Cat in a third, and she was the variable that kept shifting the totals.

He had every reason to let this building burn. He had been beaten in these halls — I'd seen the scars, heard the way his voice changed when he talked about the east wing, the dormitories, the rooms where they'd broken him and then taught him to break others. He had bought Blackwood with the explicit purpose of destroying it. Watching it burn was supposed to be vindication. Watching it burn was supposed to be the end of something.
Walking back in was supposed to be impossible.

"I'm not asking you to forgive him," I said, though that wasn't the right framing at all. I wasn't asking. I wasn't even asking, and we both knew it. I was telling him what I needed, and hoping — praying — that it would be enough.

Heath's jaw worked. A tendon stood out in his neck. He looked at the burning corridor, and I saw his whole body go rigid with something that wasn't fear. It was memory. It was the body remembering what the mind had tried to bury, the muscle and bone and nerve preserving what the conscious mind could not afford to hold.
Then he looked at me.

Really looked. Not at the strategy, not at the variables, not at the cost. At me. At the person standing in the smoke with soot on her face and ash in her hair, asking him to do the one thing he'd sworn he'd never do.
What he saw in my face, I don't know. I wasn't trying to look any particular way. I wasn't performing. I was just — there. Raw. Afraid. Unwilling to walk away and unable to go in alone.
He took his jacket off.

He wrapped it around his left hand, the fabric thick wool, dark with rain and snowmelt from the blizzard outside. He pulled the sleeves tight around his wrist and tested the coverage — knuckles, palm, forearm — with the methodical attention of someone preparing for something terrible.
Then he walked into the fire.

I watched him go. The smoke swallowed him in three steps, the orange glow outlining his silhouette like a saint's halo — and wasn't that a joke, Heath Cruz lit up like something holy when nothing about this was holy at all. The corridor accepted him like a mouth accepting food, the flames bending and reaching toward him, and then he was gone.
And I was alone.
The seconds were hours.

I stood at the threshold, one hand on the doorframe, and the heat hit me in waves — a pulse, a heartbeat, the building alive and angry and trying to push me back. The wood under my fingers was hot enough to hurt but not hot enough to burn, and I held on anyway because letting go felt like drifting.

The smoke billowed out in thick curtains, rolling along the ceiling and then collapsing earthward, and every breath I took was a negotiation with my lungs. The taste was chemical — not just wood smoke but burning varnish, melting plastic, something synthetic and sweet that coated my tongue and made me want to retch. The sprinklers overhead spat intermittently, droplets hitting my face, and they were warm, which was wrong in a way I couldn't articulate. Water shouldn't be warm.

A beam groaned somewhere above me. A long, low, animal sound — the building's death rattle, the skeleton starting to buckle. I looked up and saw cracks spider-webbing across the plaster ceiling, dark lines spreading like veins, and I understood that I was standing under a structure that was actively deciding whether or not to fall on me.

I couldn't go in. I'd be a liability — another body to drag out, another weight to slow Heath down. I was small and I was fast but I wasn't strong, not like him, and in that corridor the only currency that mattered was the ability to carry another human being through fire. I didn't have it.

I couldn't leave. Leaving meant abandoning both of them — Heath, who'd walked into the building that had destroyed him because I asked him to, and Julian, who'd walked into it because he couldn't stop being good. The thought of walking out into the snow alone, of driving away with the fire behind me and their names on my conscience, was physically repellent. My body wouldn't do it. My legs locked.
So I stood at the threshold and I prayed.

Not to God. I didn't believe in God — had stopped believing somewhere between my father's abandonment and Blackwood's careful education, between the beatings Heath endured and the calculations I was taught to perform on human lives. God was a story they told us in chapel to keep us compliant, and I'd learned to see the strings.

I prayed to the universe. To probability. To the cold mathematics that had governed every decision I'd ever made, every risk assessment, every calculation of acceptable loss. I prayed the way I calculated — with precision, with desperation, with the part of me that refused to accept that the numbers could come out wrong.
Let them come out.
The beam groaned again. Plaster dust sifted down, catching in my eyelashes.
Let them come out.
The fire roared. Something collapsed deeper in the building, a thunderous crump that vibrated through the soles of my shoes and up through my shins.
Let them come out.

The wind from the blizzard pushed through the broken windows at the far end of the corridor, a cold blade cutting through the heat, and for a moment the smoke cleared enough that I could see ten feet into the east wing instead of three. Ten feet of charred wainscoting and burning carpet and a world that had no business existing inside a building where children had once slept.
Then the smoke closed again, and I was blind.

I counted seconds. One-Mississippi, two-Mississippi, the way I'd learned as a child, back when the world was simple enough that time could be measured in nursery rhythms. I got to forty-seven before I heard something.
A sound. Not the fire. Not the building. A sound that was human — coughing, deep and wet and desperate, the sound of lungs trying to expel what they'd inhaled.
Then figures.

Two shapes in the smoke, one supporting the other. One tall, one limp. The orange light caught Heath's profile first — ash-gray with soot, eyes streaming, jaw clenched so tight I could see the muscles jumping even through the smoke. He had Julian over his shoulder in a fireman's carry, and he was moving, but barely. Each step was a negotiation with the ground, a deliberate placement of one foot and then the other, the deliberate progress of someone who had decided to walk and was refusing to let the building take that decision back.

Julian's arm dangled against Heath's back, swinging with each step, and I could see the fingers were moving — barely, a slow curl and uncurl, the body's last protest against unconsciousness. He was alive.
I ran.

The heat hit me again but I didn't care. I closed the distance in four steps and got my arm under Julian's other side, taking half his weight, and Heath made a sound — relief, maybe, or just the exhale of someone who'd been holding more than he could carry.
"He's breathing," Heath said, and his voice was gone now, just air and damage. "Barely."

Julian's head lolled toward me. His face was pale beneath the soot, that particular gray-white of someone who'd been breathing poison, and his eyes were half-open — not seeing, not tracking, just open, like the body hadn't gotten the message yet that it was supposed to shut down. His lips were cracked. His breathing was a thin wheeze, a sound like air through a broken reed, and I could feel his ribs moving under my arm — fast, too fast, the body panicking.
"Move," I said.
We moved.

Together, half-carrying, half-dragging, we pulled Julian down the corridor toward the exit. The floor was buckling under our feet — the parquet warping from the heat, planks curling upward like pages of a book left in the rain. I stumbled once, caught myself on Heath's shoulder, kept going. The wind from outside was stronger now, the blizzard pushing through the broken windows with increasing force, and I could feel the cold on my face, the juxtaposition of fire-behind and blizzard-ahead so extreme that my body couldn't calibrate, couldn't decide whether it was burning or freezing.
We reached the door.
The night hit us like a slap.

Cold air — clean, clean, so clean my lungs spasmed trying to accept it after the poisoned atmosphere inside. Snow was falling horizontally, driving in sheets that stung exposed skin and melted on contact with anything warm. The courtyard was white and grey and black, the snow lit orange by the fire behind us, and the world was so impossibly large after the corridor that I felt vertigo, the sudden unmooring of emerging from confinement into open space.

We dragged Julian into the snow. He coughed — a deep, racking sound that bent him double even between us — and then he was breathing, really breathing, pulling air in great desperate gulps like a drowning person breaking the surface.
Heath lowered him to the ground and straightened, and I saw his arm.

The left arm, the one he'd wrapped in his jacket. The wool had burned through in patches, and the skin beneath was red and blistered, raw in places where the fabric had surrendered. He was cradling it against his chest, and his face had gone still in a way I recognized — the deliberate blankness of someone in significant pain who refuses to acknowledge it.
"You're hurt," I said.
"I'm fine."
"You're not fine."
He didn't answer. He was looking back at the building.

I followed his gaze. The east wing was fully visible now, and it was a furnace. Orange light poured from every window, every crack, every gap in the stonework, and the flames were climbing — reaching up through the roof, eating through the timbers, consuming the place where Julian had almost died and where Heath had been broken and remade into something hard and sharp.
As we watched, the roof of the east wing buckled.

It didn't collapse so much as fold — a slow, almost graceful surrender, the ridge line dipping and then crashing inward, sending a column of sparks and smoke twenty feet into the night sky. The sound was enormous, a grinding roar that vibrated in my chest, and the shockwave of heat that followed pushed against us even from thirty feet away, warm air washing over my face like a breath.
Gone. The east wing was gone.
And we were still here.

Julian coughed again, and his eyes focused for the first time — finding my face, then Heath's, then the burning building. He tried to speak and couldn't, his throat too damaged for words, but his expression said enough. Confusion. Gratitude. The slow, dawning horror of someone realizing how close he'd come.
Heath sat down in the snow beside him, cradling his burned arm, and said nothing.

I stood between them — the villain who'd walked into fire because the woman he loved asked him to, the saint who'd almost burned because he couldn't stop being good, and me. The weapon. The one who'd made the choice.

The snow kept falling. The fire kept burning. And somewhere in the darkness beyond the orange glow, the blizzard was building — the wind rising, the temperature dropping, the storm closing its fist around the east coast with the slow, patient certainty of something that could not be negotiated with.
We were out.
But the night was just beginning.
